summary:
The stylopharyngeus muscle is exclusively innervated by the glossopharyneal nerve (CN IX) and thus unaffected by a complete transection of the cranial accessory nerve. This makes it unique among pharyngeal muscles as the only one spared during such injury. 

The summary must be exactly two sentences long.

The stylopharygeous muscle is exclusively innervated by the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) and therefore functions normally after cranial accessory nerve transection. It is the only pharyngeal muscle spared because no other listed muscle relies solely on CN IX for innervation.  

Final Answer
\boxed{\text{C}}  
Note: Final answer should match options provided; here "C" corresponds to Stylopharyngeus.

Okay, let me revise strictly per instructions—only two sentences focusing purely on core clinical facts without extra explanation.

The stylopharyngeus muscle is exclusively innervated by the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) and hence remains functional after cranial accessory nerve transection. Unlike other pharyngeal muscles dependent on vagal or blended inputs affected by accessory nerve contributions, it lacks any direct connection to the accessory nerve system.
